steps.title: Identify the two parts of the sentence
steps.content: The passage makes a general claim — judicial independence has become more prevalent among constitutions — and then introduces Poland’s 1997 constitution as a specific example. The blank must punctuate the end of the general claim and attach “Enacted in 1997” to Poland’s constitution, not to “constitutions” generally.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — constitutions enacted in 1997;: makes “enacted in 1997” a restrictive modifier on “constitutions,” incorrectly implying only 1997 constitutions have this feature. ✗
  • Choice B — constitutions, enacted in 1997;: a comma before a semicolon creates disruptive punctuation; “enacted in 1997” still modifies “constitutions” rather than Poland’s constitution. ✗
  • Choice C — constitutions, enacted in 1997,: two commas around “enacted in 1997” form a comma splice between two independent clauses with no separating semicolon. ✗
  • Choice D — constitutions; enacted in 1997,: the semicolon ends the general claim; “Enacted in 1997” then opens the next independent clause as a participial phrase modifying Poland’s constitution, and the comma after it correctly sets off that introductory phrase. ✓
That is the correct answer.
